nativeRange.habitat: Intertidal mudflats and mangrove estuaries; spends the majority of time out of water on exposed mud, retreating to burrows at low tide. Inhabits the intertidal zone of West African coastlines from Senegal to Angola.

decorPreferences: large sloping land area (at least 50% of tank footprint) of moistened sandy-clay substrate
shallow brackish water section (5–10 cm deep) for bathing and gill-wetting
mangrove roots or driftwood for basking and territorial marking
tight-fitting lid essential — strong escape artists
high humidity maintained in air space above water and land (80–90%)
burrowing substrate (soft mud or sand-clay mix, minimum 10 cm deep) for territorial burrows
gentle filtration — low water flow; sump or hang-on filter kept to minimal surface agitation

diet.notes: Obligate insectivore/invertivore in the wild; primarily live invertebrates. Staple diet of live crickets, mealworms, waxworms, earthworms, fruit flies (Drosophila), and other appropriately sized live insects. May accept frozen bloodworm or frozen brine shrimp offered via tongs directly on land. Rarely accepts sinking pellets — live or fresh prey is strongly preferred. Gut-load insects with calcium-rich food before feeding. Feed on the land surface, not in the water column; mudskippers do not naturally feed underwater.

goodWith: conspecifics in large paludariums with multiple clearly defined territories (with caution)
fiddler crabs (similar biotope, similar brackish requirements — monitor closely)
mangrove killies in the water section (if sufficient land space reduces aggression)
avoidWith: peaceful community fish — incompatible environment requirements
invertebrates that will be eaten (shrimp, small crabs)
any fish requiring fully freshwater or fully marine conditions
slow-moving bottom fish that cannot retreat from territorial displays

imageCandidates.notes: Periophthalmus barbarus on mudflat, clear dorsal fin display visible. Good representative image.
FishBase species photo of P. barbarus. Check current availability on FishBase species page.

summary: The Atlantic Mudskipper is a unique amphibious goby from West African intertidal mangroves that spends most of its time on land, breathing through moist skin and buccal cavity. Requires a specialist paludarium with brackish water and substantial terrestrial space — not a freshwater fish despite trade labelling.
careNotes: Periophthalmus barbarus is one of the most distinctive fish available in the aquarium trade — it is genuinely amphibious, spending the majority of its life out of water on exposed mudflats and mangrove margins, returning to water primarily to wet its gills, reproduce, and thermoregulate. It breathes through its skin and buccal cavity when on land, provided these surfaces remain moist; a humid air space (80–90% relative humidity) above the land area is therefore non-negotiable. A paludarium design is mandatory: roughly 50% of the total tank footprint must be a sloping, accessible land area composed of soft mud or sandy-clay substrate at least 10 cm deep to allow burrow construction. The water section should be shallow (5–10 cm), brackish (SG 1.005–1.010, equivalent to approximately 6.5–13 ppt), and warm (24–30°C). A tight-fitting lid is essential — mudskippers are powerful jumpers and skilled climbers, easily escaping standard aquarium hoods. Mudskippers sold in the trade are sometimes mislabelled as freshwater fish; they are not freshwater-tolerant long-term and will decline in pure freshwater. Maintain brackish conditions from the outset. Territorial aggression is intense: males perform erect-dorsal-fin threat displays, chase, and bite conspecifics. A single specimen or a proven pair with a very large footprint (150 L minimum, 200+ L preferred) is strongly advised. Multiple specimens require clearly partitioned territories, and even then aggression monitoring is essential. Feed exclusively on the land surface — mudskippers do not naturally hunt underwater. Live insects (crickets, waxworms, fruit flies, earthworms) are the preferred staple; accept frozen bloodworm offered with tongs on land. Gut-load feeder insects for nutritional completeness. Water quality in the small water section degrades quickly; frequent partial water changes and efficient filtration (with minimal current) are required.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is rarely achieved and is considered advanced to specialist level. In the wild, males excavate burrows at the waterline and court females with dramatic fin-erecting and jumping displays. Eggs are deposited in air pockets within burrows and guarded by the male, who actively aerates the chamber by gulping air. Replicating this in captivity requires a very large paludarium with deep, packable substrate, stable brackish water at the burrow entrance, and significant male territorial space. Fry are pelagic and require live foods (rotifers, baby brine shrimp) at hatching. Successfully captive-bred specimens have been reported only by dedicated specialist keepers.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Periophthalmus barbarus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (West Africa, intertidal mangroves), water parameters (temperature 24–30°C, pH 7.5–8.5, salinity 1.004–1.010 SG), adult size (to 15 cm TL), paludarium requirements, diet (live invertebrates/insects), temperament (highly territorial), and breeding overview.

additional.name: FishBase — Periophthalmus barbarus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Oxudercidae, order Gobiiformes), adult size (to 25 cm TL reported — maximum wild size; aquarium specimens typically 10–15 cm), native range (Senegal to Angola, Atlantic West African coast), habitat (intertidal mudflats and mangrove), and diet (invertebrates, insects, small fish). Confirmed euryhaline classification.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Mudskipper Care
additional.notes: Detailed husbandry guide; confirmed paludarium requirements (50% land, 50% shallow brackish water), humidity needs (80–90%), live insect diet, escape-proofing requirements, and aggressive territorial behaviour. Noted mislabelling as freshwater fish in trade.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Brackish Fish Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary brackish-fish trade and husbandry context; confirmed brackish salinity requirements and noted mudskipper specialist-only status. General brackish guide rather than species-specific.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Mudskippers: Amphibious Wonders
additional.notes: Species overview article covering amphibious biology, gill/skin respiration mechanism, West African intertidal biotope, territorial behaviour and male burrow-guarding breeding biology, and specialist aquarium requirements. Confirmed insectivore diet and paludarium setup guidance.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely achieved in captivity. Requires a large paludarium with deep packable substrate, stable brackish water at burrow entrances, and significant male territorial space. Males excavate burrows, court with fin-erecting displays, and guard eggs in air-pocket chambers within the burrow. Fry require live rotifers and baby brine shrimp.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Requires paludarium (half-water half-land) setup with brackish water (SG 1.005-1.010), terrestrial space, heated humid air, and live insect/feeder diet. Not freshwater despite being sold so. Specialist amphibious-setup commitment.
